summary: DHH applies Kierkegaard's either/or philosophy to Twitter's potential acquisition by Elon Musk. He argues that both outcomes—selling or not selling—will lead to regret. Under current management, Twitter will continue increasing censorship and policing dissenting opinions. Under Musk, the platform might become even more toxic by maximizing engagement through algorithmic manipulation. DHH sees this as a lose-lose scenario with no good options.
key_points: Kierkegaard's either/or framework applies perfectly to the Musk-Twitter acquisition dilemma
Current Twitter management trends toward increasing censorship and suppression of dissenting views
Musk's Twitter would likely maximize engagement, amplifying algorithmic toxicity and division
Removing overt political content moderation won't make the platform less political
money_quotes: Sell Twitter to Musk, and you'll regret it. Don't sell Twitter to Musk, and you'll regret that too.
Many of life's grand dilemmas have only regrettable choices. Yet we spend much of our time trying to argue and act as if otherwise.
More bans, more suppression, more policing of thought divergent from The Consensus Opinion.
Twitter made in Musk's image might well "unlock the potential" of the site, and thus infect even more people with the algorithmic darkness and division that maximizing for engagement brings.
Less overt political gerrymandering of the Overton window will not make for a less political site.
Either way, we'll all regret it.
key_insight: The Twitter acquisition debate is a false choice between two bad outcomes—continued censorship under current management or amplified algorithmic toxicity under Musk—and neither path leads anywhere good.
